---
title: "Post template — mixing diagrams, plots, and prose"
description: "A starter template demonstrating Mermaid, Python plots, callouts, and math in one post."
author: "Malte Nalenz"
date: "2026-05-20"
categories: [template, examples]
image: thumbnail.png
image-alt: "Example thumbnail for the post"
draft: true
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

A short lead paragraph that summarizes the post in two or three sentences.
This sits above the first heading and shows up as the listing preview when
the `description` field is empty.

## A simple diagram

Mermaid diagrams render natively in Quarto — no extra setup needed.

```{mermaid}
---
config:
  look: handDrawn
  theme: neutral
  layout: elk
---
flowchart TD
    A{large \n number TS?} -->|yes| B{Share similar pattern}
    A -->|no| C{Interpretability \n required?}
    C -->|no| D[Pretrained Transformer]
    C -->|yes| E[Classic Time Series]
    B -->|no| D
    B -->|yes| F[Global ML forecasting]
```

Use Mermaid for pipelines, sequence diagrams, decision trees, or anything
otherwise diagrammable. Full syntax reference:
[mermaid-js.github.io](https://mermaid-js.github.io/).

## A Python plot

Code cells execute at render time. The first run can be slow; subsequent
renders use Quarto's freeze cache (`_freeze/`), so unchanged posts are not
re-executed in CI.

```{python}
#| label: fig-forecast
#| fig-cap: "Synthetic demand series with a simple rolling-mean forecast."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
t = pd.date_range("2025-01-01", periods=120, freq="D")
trend = np.linspace(100, 140, len(t))
seasonal = 8 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(t)) / 7)
noise = rng.normal(0, 4, len(t))
y = trend + seasonal + noise

series = pd.Series(y, index=t, name="demand")
forecast = series.rolling(window=14, min_periods=1).mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(series.index, series.values, label="actual", lw=1, alpha=0.6)
ax.plot(forecast.index, forecast.values, label="14-day rolling mean", lw=2)
ax.set_xlabel("date")
ax.set_ylabel("demand")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The figure above is referenced as [@fig-forecast] via its `label`. Cross-references
work for figures, tables, equations, and sections.

## Prose with math and emphasis

Inline math: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} y_{t-i}$.

Display math:

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left| \frac{y_t - \hat{y}_t}{y_t} \right|
$$ {#eq-mape}

Refer to equations with [@eq-mape].

## Callouts for asides

::: {.callout-note}
Use callouts to highlight context, caveats, or side-notes without breaking
the flow of the main argument.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## When rolling means break
A rolling-mean baseline is only honest on stationary series. If trend or
regime shifts dominate, it under-reacts and the bias compounds.
:::

## Tables

| Method                 | MAPE | Notes                                  |
|------------------------|-----:|----------------------------------------|
| Naive (last value)     | 12.4 | Strong baseline; surprisingly hard to beat. |
| Rolling mean (14d)     |  8.1 | Smooths weekly seasonality.            |
| Seasonal naive (7d)    |  6.7 | Captures the dominant weekly cycle.    |
| LightGBM with lags     |  4.9 | Best with feature engineering.         |

: Forecast accuracy on the synthetic series above. {#tbl-results}

See [@tbl-results] for the comparison.

## Wrapping up

Close with the takeaway: one or two sentences on what the reader should
remember. Link to the next post or a related project if useful.
